children.name: Setophaga
children.description: The typical wood-warblers — a large and diverse genus of small, insectivorous passerines that forms the core of the Parulidae. Males in breeding plumage display bold patterns of yellow, black, white, blue, and chestnut; females and non-breeding birds are more subdued. They are among the most sought-after birds by North American birders during spring migration, when males sing from treetops in brilliant breeding colours.

children.name: Setophaga ruticilla
children.description: A hyperactive warbler that constantly fans its colourful tail and droops its wings, flashing orange and black patches to flush insects from foliage. The male is black with vivid orange patches on the tail, wings, and sides; the female is grey with yellow patches. It breeds across much of North America and winters in the Caribbean, Central America, and northern South America.

children.name: Setophaga petechia
children.description: The most widespread Setophaga warbler, with 43 subspecies across the Americas. The male is brilliant yellow with fine chestnut streaks on the breast and a sweet, whistled 'sweet-sweet-sweet-I'm-so-sweet' song. It inhabits riparian thickets, willow swamps, and gardens across North America, and mangroves in the Caribbean and Central America, where resident subspecies are found year-round.

children.name: Setophaga coronata
children.description: One of the most abundant and widespread North American wood-warblers, distinguished by its bright yellow rump and yellow patches on the sides. It is uniquely adapted among warblers to digest waxy fruits, allowing it to winter farther north and survive on bayberries when insects are scarce. It is often the first warbler to arrive in spring and the last to leave in autumn.

children.name: Setophaga caerulescens
children.description: A strikingly dimorphic warbler: the male is deep blue above with a black face, throat, and flanks contrasting with a white belly; the female is plain olive-brown with a distinctive white wing patch. It breeds in the understory of mature deciduous and mixed forests in eastern North America and winters in Caribbean forests, especially in Cuba and Jamaica.

children.name: Setophaga fusca
children.description: Arguably the most brilliantly coloured of all North American warblers: the male has a flaming orange throat, neck, and crown set against a black head and cheek, with white wing patches. It breeds in mature coniferous and mixed forests of northeastern North America, favouring large trees for its high canopy foraging. Its thin, wiry, high-pitched song seems to ascend past the range of human hearing.

children.name: Setophaga americana
children.description: A small, dainty warbler with a blue-grey head and back, two bold white wing bars, and a yellow patch on the back. The male has a chestnut band across the yellow breast. It breeds in mature forests rich in epiphytic moss and lichen, particularly Usnea and Tillandsia, in which it nests. Its buzzy, rising trill that ends with an abrupt note is a distinctive sound of eastern North American woodlands.

children.name: Setophaga magnolia
children.description: A striking warbler with a bold pattern: yellow underparts heavily streaked with black, a white supercilium, and a distinctive white band across the tail visible in flight. The male has a black mask bordered by yellow. It breeds in young coniferous forests across Canada and the northeastern United States and winters in the Caribbean and Central America. Its emphatic 'weeta-weeta-wichee' song is easily recognised.

children.name: Geothlypis
children.description: The yellowthroats — a genus of stocky, skulking warblers of dense, wet vegetation. Males are distinguished by a bold black mask across the face, bordered above by a white or grey band. They are more often heard than seen, delivering a loud, repetitive 'wichity-wichity-wichity' song from the depths of cattail marshes and brushy swamps.

children.name: Geothlypis trichas
children.description: One of the most widespread and familiar warblers in North America, found in almost every marsh, swamp, and brushy field from Alaska to Mexico. The male is unmistakable with its bold black bandit mask bordered by white. Its cheerful 'wichity-wichity-wichity' song is a hallmark of wetland habitats, and it skulks in dense vegetation, popping up only briefly to scold observers.

children.name: Geothlypis formosa
children.description: The Kentucky Warbler is a striking yellow-and-black warbler with a bold black mask, yellow spectacles, and olive-green upperparts. It skulks in the understory of mature deciduous forests of the southeastern United States and winters in Central and northern South America.

children.name: Geothlypis philadelphia
children.description: The Mourning Warbler has a grey hood, black throat patch, and bright yellow underparts, with no eye-ring or supercilium. It breeds in dense second-growth thickets of boreal Canada and winters in Central and South America.

children.name: Geothlypis tolmiei
children.description: MacGillivray's Warbler has a grey hood, broken white eye-rings, and bright yellow underparts. It breeds in dense brushy thickets of western North America and winters in Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Protonotaria
children.description: The prothonotary warbler — a monotypic genus of cavity-nesting warbler named for the golden-yellow robes of papal notaries. It is one of only two Parulidae species that nest in tree cavities, and the only eastern North American warbler to do so. Its brilliant colouration and gentle song make it one of the most prized species among birders.

children.name: Protonotaria citrea
children.description: A stunning golden-yellow warbler with blue-grey wings and a bright orange-yellow head and breast. It is one of only two cavity-nesting warblers in North America, readily using nest boxes placed in swamps and flooded woodlands. It breeds in bottomland hardwood forests of the southeastern United States and winters in mangroves along the Caribbean coasts of Central and South America.

children.name: Seiurus
children.description: The ovenbird — a monotypic genus of large, terrestrial warbler named for its distinctive domed nest, which resembles a dutch oven. It is one of the few warblers that forages entirely on the forest floor, walking deliberately in search of invertebrates. Its loud, ringing 'teacher-TEACHER-TEACHER' song is one of the most powerful and distinctive sounds of eastern North American forests.

children.name: Seiurus aurocapilla
children.description: A thrush-like warbler of the forest floor, with an olive-brown back, heavily streaked white breast, and a distinctive orange crown bordered by black stripes. It builds a remarkable domed nest of leaves and grasses on the ground with a side entrance, resembling a Dutch oven. Its song, a series of emphatic 'teacher' notes increasing in volume, carries remarkably far through the woodland understory.

children.name: Mniotilta
children.description: The black-and-white warbler — a distinctive monotypic genus with a unique foraging behaviour that sets it apart from all other Parulidae. It creeps along tree trunks and branches like a nuthatch, probing bark crevices for hidden insects. Its black-and-white streaked plumage resembles that of a tiny woodpecker, and its squeaky, wheel-like song is a familiar sound of North American woodlands.

children.name: Mniotilta varia
children.description: The only warbler in North America that forages in the manner of a nuthatch or creeper, investigating bark crevices on trunks and large branches for hidden insects. Its bold black-and-white longitudinal streaking is unique among North American warblers. Its thin, squeaky 'wee-see-wee-see-wee-see' song sounds like a slowly turning wheel and is a common sound in mature eastern deciduous forests.

children.name: Parkesia
children.description: The waterthrushes — a small genus of large, terrestrial warblers restricted to wet habitats. They resemble thrushes in build and behaviour, with heavily streaked underparts and a habit of bobbing their rear body. They walk on the ground along streams and in swamps, flipping dead leaves in search of insects, and are among the most secretive of North American warblers despite their size.

children.name: Parkesia noveboracensis
children.description: A large, thrush-like warbler with heavily streaked yellowish underparts and a prominent white supercilium. It bobs its tail constantly as it walks along the edges of streams, swamps, and beaver ponds in the boreal forests of North America. Its loud, ringing 'sweet-sweet-sweet' song accelerates and descends in pitch, echoing through northern wetlands. It winters in mangroves and swamp forests from Mexico to the Amazon.

children.name: Cardellina
children.description: A small genus of colourful warblers with predominantly red or yellow plumage, including the redstarts and the Canada Warbler. They are active, acrobatic foragers of montane and boreal forests across the Americas.

children.name: Cardellina canadensis
children.description: The Canada Warbler is a striking blue-grey warbler with bright yellow underparts and a distinctive 'necklace' of black spots across the breast. It breeds in moist mixed forests of boreal Canada and the northeastern United States and winters in the Andes of South America.

children.name: Cardellina pusilla
children.description: Wilson's Warbler is a small, bright yellow warbler with a distinctive black cap in males. It breeds in dense willow and alder thickets near water across northern North America and winters from Mexico to Central America.

children.name: Leiothlypis
children.description: A genus of small, plain-faced warblers with thin pointed bills and predominantly olive or grey upperparts. They forage deliberately in trees and shrubs, and are among the most common Neotropical migrants in North America.

children.name: Leiothlypis peregrina
children.description: The Tennessee Warbler is a plain olive-green warbler with white underparts, a white supercilium, and a thin pointed bill. It breeds in boreal forests of Canada and winters in Central and South America, where it feeds extensively on nectar and insects.

children.name: Leiothlypis celata
children.description: The Orange-crowned Warbler is a drab olive-green warbler with faint streaking on the underparts and a concealed orange crown patch. It is one of the hardiest warblers, wintering farther north than most and arriving early on its breeding grounds across northern North America.

children.name: Leiothlypis ruficapilla
children.description: The Nashville Warbler has a grey head, white eye-ring, yellow underparts, and olive-green upperparts. It breeds in open woodlands and bogs across Canada and the northern United States and winters in Mexico and Central America.

children.name: Leiothlypis virginiae
children.description: Virginia's Warbler is a small, grey warbler with a yellow rump and breast, a white eye-ring, and a chestnut crown patch. It breeds on dry, scrubby mountain slopes of the southwestern United States and winters in western Mexico.

children.name: Oporornis
children.description: A monotypic genus of secretive, skulking warbler with a preference for wet boreal forests and bogs. It is more often heard than seen, delivering its distinctive song from deep within dense spruce and tamarack stands.

children.name: Oporornis agilis
children.description: The Connecticut Warbler is a large, olive-brown warbler with a grey hood, bold white eye-ring, and yellow underparts. It is a secretive bird of boreal bogs and muskegs, more often identified by its loud, ringing 'chirp-chirp-chirp-chirp-chirp' song than by sight.

children.name: Vermivora
children.description: A small genus of brightly coloured warblers with bold wing patterns and relatively large bills. They are among the most sought-after warblers by birders due to their striking beauty and the conservation concern surrounding some species.

children.name: Vermivora cyanoptera
children.description: The Blue-winged Warbler is a bright yellow warbler with blue-grey wings marked by two white wing bars and a bold black line through the eye. It breeds in early-successional habitats across eastern North America and winters in Central America.

children.name: Vermivora chrysoptera
children.description: The Golden-winged Warbler is a stunning warbler with a golden crown and wing patch, black throat and cheek, and grey body. It breeds in early-successional habitats of the Great Lakes region and Appalachians, and is one of the most rapidly declining songbirds in North America.

children.name: Myioborus
children.description: The whitestarts or redstarts — a genus of brightly coloured warblers with vivid red, yellow, and black plumage and distinctive white outer tail feathers that are constantly fanned. They are active, acrobatic foragers of montane forests from the southwestern United States through the Andes.

children.name: Myioborus pictus
children.description: The Painted Redstart is a striking black warbler with a vivid red belly and breast, white wing patches, and white outer tail feathers constantly fanned. It inhabits oak and pine-oak woodlands of the southwestern United States and Mexico.

children.name: Myioborus miniatus
children.description: The Slate-throated Redstart has a slate-grey head and throat, bright yellow underparts, and chestnut crown. It is a common inhabitant of montane forests from Mexico through Central America to the Andes of South America.

children.name: Myioborus ornatus
children.description: The Golden-fronted Redstart has a bright golden-yellow forehead and crown, black mask, yellow underparts, and grey upperparts. It is found in montane cloud forests of Colombia, Ecuador, and northern Peru.
